Summary: This study investigates the potential of radiomics features extracted from normal-appearing white matter (NAWM) to predict the progression of multiple sclerosis (MS) on MRI. The authors trained a machine learning model (XGBoost) to differentiate between "prelesion" areas and control samples in NAWM, achieving high accuracy in internal testing and moderate accuracy in external validation using the MSSEG2 dataset. The study suggests that radiomics features could serve as imaging biomarkers for MS progression.

Item1.reason: The study mentions adherence to the ARISE guideline and the Checklist for Artificial Intelligence in Medical Imaging.

Item2.reason: The study explicitly defines inclusion and exclusion criteria for patient selection, aiming for a representative study population.

Item3.reason: The reference standard (radiologic progression) is defined according to the MAGNIMS consensus guidelines, which is a well-established standard in MS imaging.

Item4.reason: The study uses data from an internal cohort and an external validation cohort (MSSEG2) which includes multiple institutions.

Item5.reason: The imaging data source (MRI) and sequences (3D T2-FLAIR) are commonly used in clinical practice for MS diagnosis and monitoring.

Item6.reason: The study reports the image acquisition parameters for the 3D T2-FLAIR sequence, enhancing the replicability of the method.

Item7.reason: The study projects new lesion segmentations backward in time to the prior MR imaging study, which is appropriate to validate the presence or absence of target conditions of the radiomics analysis at the moment of the diagnostic imaging exams.

Condition1.reason: The study includes segmentation of MS lesions and normal-appearing white matter (NAWM).

Condition2.reason: The study uses DeepMedic for initial automated segmentation, which is then manually corrected.

Item8.reason: The study describes the segmentation methodology, including the use of DeepMedic for initial segmentation and manual correction by radiologists.

Item9.reason: The study mentions manual correction of the automated segmentation, but does not provide a formal evaluation of the fully automated segmentation before the manual correction.

Item10.reason: The test set segmentation masks are produced by radiologists, not by a single reader or automated tool.

Condition3.reason: The study extracts hand-crafted radiomic features using PyRadiomics.

Item11.reason: The study mentions coregistration of images and excludes shape-related radiomic features because the shapes of the control segmentations were dependent on the original lesion segmentations.

Item12.reason: The study uses PyRadiomics for feature extraction and mentions the version number.

Item13.reason: The study describes the feature types (radiomics features) and mentions that shape-related features were excluded. The study also provides a link to the code used for the experiments.

Condition4.reason: The study uses tabular data in the form of radiomic features.

Condition5.reason: The study does not use an end-to-end deep learning pipeline.

Item14.reason: The study does not explicitly mention the removal of non-robust features.

Item15.reason: The study does not explicitly mention the removal of redundant features.

Item16.reason: The study does not provide a justification for the appropriateness of dimensionality compared to data size.

Item17.reason: The study does not use an end-to-end deep learning pipeline.

Item18.reason: The study mentions training, test, and external validation sets. The code is available on github.

Item19.reason: The study mentions failure analysis on external validation showed that in many instances, a potential cause for misclassified cases was misregistration of images rather than the machine learning model.

Item20.reason: The study reports accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score as performance evaluation metrics.

Item21.reason: The study does not include uncertainty measures such as confidence intervals or standard deviations.

Item22.reason: The study does not assess the calibration of the final model.

Item23.reason: The study does not compare the performance of uni-parametric imaging with multi-parametric imaging.

Item24.reason: The study compares the performance of the radiomics-based model with a baseline model using only the mean intensity value of the segmented regions.

Item25.reason: The study compares the performance of the radiomics-based model with a baseline model using only the mean intensity value of the segmented regions.

Item26.reason: The model is tested on an independent data set that is sampled from the same source as the training set.

Item27.reason: The model is tested with independent data from another institution (MSSEG2).

Item28.reason: The study does not explicitly state that the imaging, segmentation, clinical, or radiomics analysis data is shared with the public.

Item29.reason: The study provides a link to the code used for the experiments.

Item30.reason: The study does not explicitly state that the final model is shared in the form of a raw model file or as a ready-to-use system.
